elements.sent-2411-001: Calculations of the two-loop electron self-energy for the $1S$ Lamb shift are reported, performed to all orders in the nuclear binding strength parameter $\Za$ (where $Z$ is the nuclear charge number and $\alpha$ is the fine structure constant).
elements.sent-2411-002: Our approach allows calculations to be extended to nuclear charges lower than previously possible and improves the numerical accuracy by more than an order of magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-003: Extrapolation of our all-order results to hydrogen yields a result twice as precise as the previously accepted value [E.~Tiesinga {\em et al. Rev.. Mod.}
elements.sent-2411-004: The resulting shift in the theoretical prediction for the $1S$-$2S$ transition frequency in hydrogen decreases the value of the Rydberg constant by one standard deviation.
elements.sent-2411-005: The Rydberg constant is one of the most precisely known physical constants.
elements.sent-2411-006: It is also the cornerstone of atomic spectroscopy, as all atomic transition frequencies are proportional to it.
elements.sent-2411-007: Any theoretical predictions of atomic transitions must be multiplied by the Rydberg constant to be compared with experimental data.
elements.sent-2411-008: The most accurate determination of the Rydberg constant today involves combining the experimental $1S$-$2S$ transition frequency of hydrogen \cite{matveev:13 , the proton charge radius extracted from muonic hydrogen spectroscopy \cite{antognini:13,pachucki:24:rmp , and the theoretical prediction for the $1S$ and $2S$ Lamb shift \cite{tiesinga:21:codata18 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Among these three components, the uncertainty in the theoretical prediction is the largest and dominates the overall uncertainty of the Rydberg constant.
elements.sent-2411-010: The two-loop electron self-energy (SESE, Fig.~\ref{fig:sese ) is arguably the most problematic QED effect in the hydrogen Lamb shift, which repeatedly pushed theoretical predictions beyond their error margins over recent decades.}
elements.sent-2411-011: In 1994, Pachucki's calculation of the SESE contribution of order $m\alpha^2(\Za)^5$ \cite{pachucki:94 revealed a significant effect that resolved\footnote{As Steven Weinberg remarked on this occasion, ``So apparently quantum electrodynamics wins again''\cite{weinberg:book .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Seven years later, another calculation by Pachucki \cite{pachucki:01:pra identified a large SESE contribution of order $m\alpha^2(\Za)^6\ln(\Za)^{-2}$, once again correcting the previous theoretical prediction \cite{eides:01 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Finally, in 2005 and 2009, nonperturbative (in $\Za$) calculations of the SESE correction were conducted for hydrogen-like ions, but only for $Z\ge 10$ \cite{yerokhin:05:sese,yerokhin:09:sese .}
elements.sent-2411-014: These calculations accounted for higher orders in $\Za$ that are typically inaccessible by other means.
elements.sent-2411-015: Their results were successfully validated by experiments in the high-$Z$ region \cite{beiersdorfer:98,beiersdorfer:05,yerokhin:06:prl , but required extrapolation to offer insights for hydrogen.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \centerline{ \columnwidth{!}{ \includegraphics{sese.pdf} }} \caption{ Feynman diagrams representing the two-loop electron self-energy. The double line denotes the electron in the presence of the binding nuclear field; the wavy line denotes the exchange of a virtual photon. \label{fig:sese}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Extrapolations of the nonperturbative results towards $Z = 1$ reported in Refs.~\cite{yerokhin:05:sese,yerokhin:09:sese revealed some tension with predictions of the $\Za$ expansion \cite{pachucki:03:prl .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, it was argued \cite{karshenboim:19:sese,yerokhin:18:hydr that this could be plausibly attributed to higher-order $\Za$ contributions.}
elements.sent-2411-018: So, the presently accepted value for hydrogen \cite{tiesinga:21:codata18 was obtained by assuming the consistency of the $\Za$-expansion and the all-order results.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The optimistic error obtained in this manner nevertheless constitutes one of the two primary theoretical uncertainties in the hydrogen Lamb shift.
elements.sent-2411-020: In this Letter we demonstrate that the enhanced precision of numerical calculations challenges the assumed consistency between the nonperturbative and $\Za$-expansion results.
elements.sent-2411-021: Consequently, we find that the theoretical prediction for the hydrogen Lamb shift needs to be once again reexamined, with implications for the Rydberg constant.
elements.sent-2411-022: Previous nonperturbative calculations of the SESE correction \cite{yerokhin:05:sese,yerokhin:09:sese were performed for hydrogen-like ions with $Z\ge 10$.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Technical difficulties of these computations increased rapidly as $Z$ decreased, becoming overwhelming for the lowest nuclear charges.
elements.sent-2411-024: This was partly due to numerical cancellations that scaled as $1/Z^2$, but the main limiting factor was the partial-wave expansion of the electron propagators.
elements.sent-2411-025: This expansion is unbounded and needs to be truncated in actual computations, with the number of terms growing as $(2L)^3$ with increase of the cutoff parameter $L$.
elements.sent-2411-026: These issues prevented any further progress in improving the calculations \cite{yerokhin:05:sese,yerokhin:09:sese in the low-$Z$ region.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In this Letter, we utilize the advanced subtraction scheme suggested in Ref.~\cite{sapirstein:23 for the one-loop self-energy, which significantly improved the convergence of the partial-wave expansion in the one-loop case, see also Ref.~\cite{malyshev:24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: We generalize this approach to the two-loop case and demonstrate that it leads to a drastic improvement in the partial-wave convergence, thereby enabling a break-through in the achievable accuracy of the two-loop computations.
elements.sent-2411-029: The two-loop self-energy correction is represented by three Feynman diagrams shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:sese .}
elements.sent-2411-030: The diagram (a), known as the loop-after-loop diagram, is significantly simpler to compute than the rest and was calculated for low nuclear charges already in Refs.~\cite{mallampalli:98:prl,yerokhin:00:lalpra .}
elements.sent-2411-031: The remaining two diagrams in Fig.~\ref{fig:sese present the main computational challenge.}
elements.sent-2411-032:
The diagram in Fig.~ef{fig:sese (b) induces the so-called {\em overlapping contribution, whose formal expression is given by  egin{equation}\label{eq5} \Delta E_{O} \, = (2i\alpha)^2 \int_{C_F} d\omega_1d\omega_2 \int d fx_1 d fx_2 d fx_3 d fx_4\, D^{\mu
u}(\omega_1,x_{13})\, 
onumber 	imes D^{ho\sigma}(\omega_2,x_{24})\, {\psi}^{\dag}_a( fx_1)\, \alpha_{\mu}\, G(\vare_a-\omega_1, fx_1, fx_2)\, \alpha_{ho}\, 
onumber 	imes G(\vare_a-\omega_1-\omega_2, fx_2, fx_3)\,\alpha_{
u}\, G(\vare_a-\omega_2, fx_3, fx_4)\, 
onumber 	imes \alpha_{\sigma} \psi_a( fx_4)\,,\end{equation} where $D^{\mu
u (\omega,x)$ is the photon propagator, $\psi_a( fx)$ is the reference-state wave function with the energy $\vare_a$, $\alpha_i$ are the Dirac matrices, $G(\vare, fx_i, fx_j)$ is the Dirac-Coulomb Green function, $x_{ij = | fx_i- fx_j|$, and $C_F$ is the Feynman integration contour.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: The above formulas are formal expressions that require renormalization before any actual calculations can be performed.
elements.sent-2411-035: The renormalization scheme is based on subtracting and re-adding several first terms of the expansion of the bound-electron propagators in terms of interactions with the binding field.
elements.sent-2411-036: This procedure greatly increases the number of diagrams that need to be computed.
elements.sent-2411-037: The renormalization scheme was first formulated in Ref.~\cite{mallampalli:98:pra and fully implemented in a series of our studies \cite{yerokhin:01:sese,yerokhin:03:prl,yerokhin:06:prl .}}
elements.sent-2411-038: It is schematically depicted in Figs.~\ref{fig:nested and \ref{fig:overlap .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: The diagrams to be computed are divided into three classes: (i) those calculated in coordinate space, labeled "X," (ii) those calculated in the mixed momentum-coordinate representation, labeled "XP," and (iii) those computed in momentum space, labeled "P." Details of the calculation method are described in our previous investigations \cite{yerokhin:03:epjd,yerokhin:10:sese,yerokhin:18:sese .}
elements.sent-2411-040: The major limiting factor for the numerical accuracy achievable in this scheme is the convergence of the (double) partial-wave expansion of diagrams calculated in the coordinate space.
elements.figure-2411-002: \centerline{ \columnwidth{!}{ \includegraphics{sese_overlap4.pdf} }} \caption{ Two subtraction schemes used for calculations of the overlapping diagram: the previous scheme (without shaded diagrams) and the present scheme (with shaded diagrams), see Supplementary Material \cite{suppl}. The single solid line represents the free electron propagator, while the dashed line terminated by a stylized cross indicates the interaction with the binding nuclear field. \label{fig:nested}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \centerline{ \columnwidth{!}{ \includegraphics{sese_nested3.pdf} }} \caption{Same as Fig.~\ref{fig:nested} for the nested diagram. \label{fig:overlap}}
elements.sent-2411-041: In this Letter, we improve upon the previous subtraction scheme to achieve better partial-wave convergence for the diagrams calculated in coordinate space and the mixed momentum-coordinate representation.
elements.sent-2411-042: Following the general idea of Ref.~\cite{sapirstein:23 , we subtract and re-add diagrams containing two explicit interactions with the binding nuclear field.}
elements.sent-2411-043: There is some flexibility in choosing these subtraction diagrams since the Coulomb potentials can be "moved" to neighboring vertices without significantly affecting the behavior of the partial-wave expansion.
elements.sent-2411-044: This flexibility allows us to select subtraction diagrams that can also be computed in closed form (without any partial-wave expansion) in momentum space.
elements.sent-2411-045: The additional subtraction diagrams are depicted in Figs.~\ref{fig:nested and \ref{fig:overlap , indicated by the shaded background, and described in Supplementary Material \cite{suppl .}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: As a result, we typically achieve an improvement in the convergence of partial-wave expansions by one or two orders of magnitude in terms of achievable numerical precision.
elements.sent-2411-047: The trade-off for this improvement is the significantly larger number of diagrams that need to be computed.
elements.sent-2411-048: Numerical results of our calculation are presented in Table~\ref{tab:sese , expressed in terms of the dimensionless function $F(\Za)$, which is connected to the energy shift $\Delta E$ by \begin{equation}\Delta E = mc^2\, \frac{\alpha^2}{\pi^2} (\Za)^4\,F(\Za)\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The comparison presented in the table shows that our new results are consistent with the previous calculations \cite{yerokhin:09:sese,yerokhin:18:sese and significantly improve upon them.}
elements.sent-2411-050: For example, at $Z = 10$, the numerical accuracy is improved by a factor of 30.
elements.sent-2411-051: Additionally, we extend our computations to lower values of $Z$, down to $Z = 5$.
elements.sent-2411-052: This improvement allows for a better extrapolation towards $Z = 1$ than was previously possible, as will be discussed below.
elements.sent-2411-053: It should be noted that the implementation of the new subtraction scheme provided an additional cross-check of the numerical procedure used in our previous calculations.
elements.sent-2411-054: This is because the new subtraction diagrams are computed by two different methods: with partial-wave expansion and in the closed form.
elements.sent-2411-055: In order to compare our numerical all-order results with those obtained within the $\Za$ expansion, we need to separate out contributions of the leading $\Za$-expansion terms.
elements.sent-2411-056: The $\Za$ expansion of function $F(\Za)$ for an $S$ state has the form \begin{equation}\label{eq:1} F(\Za) = B_{40} + \ (\Za)\,B_{50} + (\Za)^2\big[ B_{63}\,L^3 \nonumber + B_{62}\,L^2 + B_{61}\,L + G_{60}(\Za)\big]\,,\end{equation} where $L = \ln (\Za)^{-2}$, $G_{60 (\Za)$ is the remainder containing all higher-order terms in $\Za$, and coefficients $B_{ij}$ can be found in Ref.~\cite{yerokhin:18:hydr .}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\label{eq:1} F(\Za) = B_{40} + \ (\Za)\,B_{50} + (\Za)^2\big[ B_{63}\,L^3 \nonumber + B_{62}\,L^2 + B_{61}\,L + G_{60}(\Za)\big]\,,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-057: The leading coefficients $B_{40}$, $B_{50}$, and $B_{63}$ have been confirmed through independent calculations and are well established, whereas the coefficients $B_{62}$ and $B_{61}$ derived by Pachucki \cite{pachucki:01:pra have yet to be independently verified.}
elements.sent-2411-058: We now use the analytical results to extract the higher-order remainder function $G_{60 (\Za)$ from our calculations (listed in Table~\ref{tab:sese ) and will return to the discussion of potential modifications to the analytical coefficients at a later stage.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: For the extrapolation of the higher-order remainder $G_{60 (\Za)$, it is important to know the functional form of its $\Za$ expansion.}
elements.sent-2411-060: It was worked out in Ref.~\cite{karshenboim:19:sese and is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:2} G_{60}(\Za) = \ B_{60} + (\Za)\big[ B_{72} \, L^2+ B_{71} \, L + B_{70}\big] \nonumber + (\Za)^2 \big[ B_{84}\,L^4 + B_{83}\,L^3 + B_{82}\,L^2 \nonumber \hspace*{2cm} + B_{81}\,L + B_{80}\big] + \ldots\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{eq:2} G_{60}(\Za) = \ B_{60} + (\Za)\big[ B_{72} \, L^2+ B_{71} \, L + B_{70}\big] \nonumber + (\Za)^2 \big[ B_{84}\,L^4 + B_{83}\,L^3 + B_{82}\,L^2 \nonumber \hspace*{2cm} + B_{81}\,L + B_{80}\big] + \ldots\,.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-061: The leading logarithmic coefficients in the above formula are known exactly, $B_{72 = -6.19408\ldots$ \cite{karshenboim:18 and $B_{84 = -7/27$ \cite{karshenboim:19:sese .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: The coefficient $B_{60}$ was partially computed in Ref.~\cite{pachucki:03:prl , with the result $B_{60 = -61.6\,(9.2)$, where the uncertainty represents the estimation of unevaluated contributions.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: For other coefficients in Eq.~(\ref{eq:2 ), Ref.~\cite{karshenboim:19:sese reported approximate constraints.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: In this Letter, we will need the constraints for the logarithmic coefficients, \begin{equation}\label{eq:3} B_{71} = -12\pm40\,,\, B_{83} = 0\pm 3.2\,,\, \nonumber B_{82} = 0\pm 50\,, \, B_{81} = 0\pm 150\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-065: Our nonperturbative results for the higher-order remainder $G_{60 (\Za)$ are plotted in Fig.~\ref{fig:ho , alongside the previously reported values from Refs.~\cite{yerokhin:09:sese,yerokhin:18:sese , and the $\Za$-expansion approximation from Ref.~\cite{karshenboim:19:sese .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: We observe that the previous all-order results fall within the shaded region of the approximate $\Za$-expansion prediction, whereas the new results clearly lie outside of that region.
elements.sent-2411-067: This indicates that the results obtained from the two methods are now in disagreement.
elements.sent-2411-068: For this reason, we will not use the analytical prediction for the $B_{60}$ coefficient together with our numerical results for extrapolation.
elements.sent-2411-069: At the same time, we will have to rely on the analytical constraints for the logarithmic coefficients as given by Eq.~(\ref{eq:3 ) in our fitting.}
elements.sent-2411-070: This is because the current numerical data cannot constrain the logarithmic coefficients $B_{71}$, $B_{83}$, etc. The details of the fitting procedure are described in Supplementary Material \cite{suppl .}
elements.sent-2411-071: The results of extrapolation of our numerical data are plotted in Fig.~\ref{fig:ho .}
elements.sent-2411-072: The structure of the fitting curve at $Z \to 0$ is determined by the analytical values of the $B_{72}$ and $B_{71}$ coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-073: For the most important cases of $Z = 0$, 1, and 2, we obtain (see also Table~I of Supplementary Material \cite{suppl ) \begin{equation}\label{eq:4} G_{60}(Z = 0) = -101.1\,(5.5)\,,\nonumber G_{60}(Z = 1) = -104.1\,(3.8)\,, G_{60}(Z = 2) = -104.6\,(2.8)\,.\nonumber\end{equation} Our result for $Z = 0$ differs by 3.7$\,\sigma$ from the analytical value of the $B_{60}$ coefficient, $-61.6\,(9.2)$ \cite{pachucki:03:prl , and for $Z = 1$, by 3.6$\,\sigma$ from the $\Za$-expansion value, $-66.8\,(9.6)$ \cite{pachucki:03:prl,karshenboim:19 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: At the same time, the present results are consistent with our previous, less precise extrapolation values $G_{60 (Z = 1) = -127\,(38)$ \cite{yerokhin:05:sese and $G_{60 (Z = 1) = -86\,(15)$ \cite{yerokhin:09:sese .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: The previously recommended value $G_{60 (Z = 1) = -75.8\,(9.4)$ from Ref.~\cite{tiesinga:21:codata18 was obtained by combining the $\Za$-expansion and the previous, less-accurate all-order results and deviates from our present value by $2.8\,\sigma$.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: It is important to note that the uncertainties of our extrapolated values rely on the analytical constraints of the logarithmic coefficients in Eq.~(\ref{eq:3 ).}
elements.sent-2411-077: If these constraints are revised, our uncertainties will have to be modified as well.
elements.sent-2411-078: The dominant contributions to the uncertainties come from the $B_{71}$ and $B_{82}$ coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-079: When speculating about possible reasons for the disagreement with the $\Za$-expansion calculations, two plausible scenarios can be considered.
elements.sent-2411-080: The first scenario involves an additional contribution to the $B_{60}$ coefficient, which has not been fully calculated yet, estimated around $\delta B_{60 \approx -40$.}
elements.sent-2411-081: The second scenario suggests a modification in the $B_{62}$ or $B_{61}$ coefficients, which have not been independently confirmed.
elements.sent-2411-082: Specifically, a 10\% adjustment in the $B_{61}$ coefficient, $\delta B_{61}\approx -5$, could restore the consistency with the nonperturbative calculations.
elements.sent-2411-083: Although our calculations are performed for the $1S$ state only, we are able to refine theoretical predictions also for other $nS$ states.
elements.sent-2411-084: This is because the normalized energy differences $\nu_{n = n^3E(nS)-E(1S)$ are currently better understood than the individual $nS$ energies \cite{tiesinga:21:codata18 .}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Consequently, the improved theoretical prediction for the $1S$ state also enhances the accuracy of the $nS$ energies via the relation $n^3E(nS) = \nu_n + E(1S)$.
elements.sent-2411-086: Turning to the experimental consequences of our calculations, the updated value of the SESE correction shifts the theoretical prediction \cite{tiesinga:21:codata18 for the $1S$-$2S$ Lamb shift in hydrogen by $-2.5$~kHz (2.0$\,\sigma$) and slightly reduces its theoretical uncertainty from $1.3$~kHz to $1.1$~kHz.}
elements.sent-2411-087: This entails the fractional shift of $-1.0 \times 10^{-12}$ in the value of the Rydberg constant.
elements.sent-2411-088: Specifically, using the SESE contribution obtained here, the remaining theory from Ref.~\cite{yerokhin:18:hydr , the value of the proton radius from Ref.~\cite{pachucki:24:rmp , and the experimental $1S$-$2S$ transition energy from Ref.~\cite{matveev:13 , we obtain the Rydberg constant $R_{\infty}$ multiplied by the speed of light $c$ as \begin{equation}\label{eq:Ry} R_{\infty}c = 3\,289\,841\,960.2449\,(23)~\rm MHz\,,\end{equation} which is smaller than the CODATA22 value \cite{CODATA2022 by 1.4 of its standard deviation.}}}}
elements.figure-2411-005: \centerline{ 1.15\columnwidth{!}{ \includegraphics{sese-2024-6.pdf} }} \caption{ Two-loop self-energy higher-order function $G_{60}(Z)$ defined in Eq.~(\ref{eq:1}), in the nonperturbative approach (the present calculations, red dots; the previous calculations \cite{yerokhin:09:sese}, brown diamonds; the fit of the present data, red dashed line and triangle), alongside with the prediction of the $\Za$-expansion approach \cite{karshenboim:19:sese} (violet square and filled area). \label{fig:ho}}
elements.sent-2411-089: In summary, we performed calculations of the two-loop electron self-energy correction to the $1S$ Lamb shift of hydrogen-like ions to all orders in the parameter $\Za$.
elements.sent-2411-090: The calculational approach developed in this Letter allowed us to improve the numerical accuracy of this effect by more than an order of magnitude and extend calculations to lower nuclear charges than previously possible.
elements.sent-2411-091: The improved numerical results break the previously assumed consistency between the all-order and the $\Za$-expansion approaches.
elements.sent-2411-092: The extrapolation of our all-order results to hydrogen revealed a disagreement of $3.6\,\sigma$ with the $\Za$-expansion prediction.
elements.sent-2411-093: The obtained result for the two-loop self-energy correction for the hydrogen is twice as precise as the currently accepted value \cite{tiesinga:21:codata18 and differs from it by $2.8\,\sigma$.}
elements.sent-2411-094: The resulting shift in the theoretical prediction for the $1S$-$2S$ transition frequency in hydrogen decreases the value of the Rydberg constant by $3.3$~kHz.
elements.sent-2411-095: The revealed disagreement calls for an independent verification of the existing $\Za$-expansion results for logarithmic contributions of order $m\alpha^2(\Za)^6$ and for calculations of the missing part of the $B_{60}$ coefficient.
elements.sent-2411-096: In addition, the analytical calculation of the $B_{71}$ coefficient is desirable, as it would ensure the correctness of the extrapolation of all-order results towards $Z = 1$ and improve its accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-097: The method developed in the present Letter opens a way for improved calculations of other two-loop QED corrections to the Lamb shift \cite{yerokhin:08:twoloop and, most importantly, the two-loop QED effects to the bound-electron $g$ factor \cite{sikora:20 , which are urgently needed for interpretation of existing experimental data \cite{morgner:23 .. \begin{widetext.}}}}
elements.section-2411-001: Supplemental Material: Details of the subtraction scheme
elements.sent-2411-098: Here we describe the calculational scheme developed in the present work which allowed us to achieve improved convergence of the partial-wave expansion.
elements.sent-2411-099: Since the detailed description of our previous approach is available in Refs.~\cite{yerokhin:03:epjd,yerokhin:10:sese,yerokhin:18:sese , here we concentrate on the new features as compared to the previous calculational scheme.}
elements.sent-2411-100: The previous scheme, referred to as the ``standard'' one in the following, is based on the separation of each of the overlapping and nested diagrams in three parts: (i) the part calculated in the coordinate space (the $M$ term), (ii) the part calculated in the mixed momentum-coordinate representation (the $P$ term), and (iii) the part calculated in the momentum space (the $F$ term).
elements.sent-2411-101: In the new calculational scheme (referred to as the ``accelerated'' one in the following), we introduce additional subtractions in the $M$ and $P$ terms and then compute the subtracted contributions in the closed form, in the momentum space.
elements.sent-2411-102: This leads to a re-definition of the separation, i.e., the $M$, $P$, and $F$ terms are replaced by the $M'$, $P'$, and $F'$ terms.
elements.sent-2411-103: In this Supplement, we will refer to formulas and figures of the main text as Eq.~(?M) and Fig.~?M, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-104: In the standard scheme, the overlapping $M$ term is obtained from Eq.~(1M) by applying the following subtractions (as shown in the uppermost line of Fig.~2M): \begin{equation}\label{eqM1} (O,M): \ \ \ G\, G\, G \ \to G\, G\, G - G\, G^{(0)}\,G^{(0)} - G^{(0)}\,G^{(0)}\,G + G ^{(0)}\,G ^{(0)}\,G ^{(0)} - G ^{(0)}\,G ^{(1)}\,G ^{(0)}\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-105: Here, the three $G$'s on the left-hand side correspond to the three Green functions in Eq.~(1M), $G^{(0)}$ denotes the free-electron Green function, and $G^{(1)}$ denotes the one-potential Green function, \begin{equation}G^{(1)}(\vare,\bfx_1,\bfx_2) = \int d\bfz\, G^{(0)}(\vare,\bfx_1,\bfz)\, V_C(z)\, G^{(0)}(\vare,\bfz,\bfx_2)\,,\end{equation} where $V_C(x) = -\Za/x$ is the nuclear Coulomb potential.
elements.sent-2411-106: For brevity, in Eq.~(\ref{eqM1 ) we omitted all arguments of the Green functions, which can be unambiguously restored from Eq.~(1M).}
elements.sent-2411-107: In order to improve the convergence of the partial-wave expansion, we introduce additional subtractions as follows (see the second line from the top in Fig.~2M): \begin{equation}\label{eqM2} (O,M'): \ \ \ G\, G\, G \ \to \ldots - 2\,V_C\, \dot{G}^{(0)} \,G^{(1)} \,G ^{(0)} - V_C\, G^{(0)} \,\ddot{G}^{(0)} \,G ^{(0)}\,V_C - V_C\, \dot{G}^{(0)} \,G^{(0)} \,\dot{G} ^{(0)}\,V_C\,,\end{equation} where $\ldots$ corresponds to the right-hand-side of Eq.~(\ref{eqM1 ), $\dot{G = -\partial /(\partial \vare)\,G(\vare)$ and $\ddot{G = (1/2)\,\partial^2 /(\partial \vare)^2\,G(\vare)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: In Eq.~(\ref{eqM2 ) and in what follows we assume the implicit ordering of radial arguments, e.g., $V_C\,G \,G \,G \,V_C$ should be understood as $V_C(x_1)\,G (\bfx_1,\bfx_2)\,G (\bfx_2,\bfx_3)\,G (\bfx_3,\bfx_4)\,V_C(x_4)$, etc..}
elements.sent-2411-109: In the standard scheme, the overlapping $P$ term takes care of the two (equivalent) subtraction terms $G\, G^{(0)}\,G^{(0)}$ and $G^{(0)}\, G^{(0)}\,G$ in Eq.~(\ref{eqM1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-110: It is defined by applying the following subtractions (see the third line from the top in Fig.~2M): \begin{equation}\label{eqP1} (O,P): \ \ \ G\, G^{(0)}\,G^{(0)} \to G\, G^{(0)}\,G^{(0)} - G^{(0)}\, G^{(0)}\,G^{(0)} - G^{(1)}\, G^{(0)}\,G^{(0)}\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-111: In the accelerated scheme, we introduce an additional subtraction and replace Eq.~(\ref{eqP1 ) by (see the third line from the top in Fig.~2M) \begin{equation}\label{eqP2} (O,P'): \ \ \ G\, G^{(0)}\,G^{(0)} \to G\, G^{(0)}\,G^{(0)} - G^{(0)}\, G^{(0)}\,G^{(0)} - G^{(1)}\, G^{(0)}\,G^{(0)} - V_C\,\ddot{G}^{(0)}\, G^{(0)}\,G^{(0)}\,V_C\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-112: The $F$ term takes care of all subtracted terms which now contain only $G^{(0)}$ (and its derivatives) and $G^{(1)}$, but not $G$'s.
elements.sent-2411-113: The additional terms subtracted in the $M$ and $P$ terms are (of course) added back and evaluated in the momentum space without any partial-wave expansion.
elements.sent-2411-114: This leads to the diagrams shown in the bottom line of Fig.~2M.
elements.sent-2411-115: Their calculation is performed by the technique developed for the $F$ term in our previous investigations \cite{yerokhin:03:epjd,yerokhin:10:sese,yerokhin:18:sese .}
elements.sent-2411-116: The calculation is long but relatively straightforward, as all of these contributions are UV finite and can be computed in $D = 4$ dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-117: In the standard scheme, the nested $M$ term is obtained from Eq.~(2M) by applying the following subtractions (see the uppermost line of Fig.~3M): \begin{equation}\label{eqM3} (N,M): \ \ \ G\, G\, G \ \to G\, G\, G - G\, G^{(0)}\,G - G\, G^{(1)}\,G \,.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}\label{eqM3} (N,M): \ \ \ G\, G\, G \ \to G\, G\, G - G\, G^{(0)}\,G - G\, G^{(1)}\,G \,.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-118: Here, we ignore the reference-state infrared (IR) divergencies present in the nested $M$ term.
elements.sent-2411-119: Treatment of the IR divergences is the same as in our previous studies, so we do not discuss it here.
elements.sent-2411-120: In the accelerated scheme, we perform an additional subtraction that greatly improves the partial-wave convergence of the nested $M$ term.
elements.sent-2411-121: Specifically, we replace Eq.~(\ref{eqM3 ) by (see the uppermost line of Fig.~3M) \begin{equation}\label{eqM4} (N,M'): \ \ \ G\, G\, G \ \to G\, G\, G - G\, G^{(0)}\,G - G\, G^{(1)}\,G - G\, V_C\, \ddot{G}^{(0)}\, V_C \,G \,.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}\label{eqM4} (N,M'): \ \ \ G\, G\, G \ \to G\, G\, G - G\, G^{(0)}\,G - G\, G^{(1)}\,G - G\, V_C\, \ddot{G}^{(0)}\, V_C \,G \,.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-122: In the standard scheme, there are two nested $P$ terms that take care of the two subtraction terms $G\, G^{(0)}\,G$ and $G\, G^{(1)}\,G$ in Eq.~(\ref{eqM3 ); they are referred to as the first and as the second, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-123: In the standard scheme, the first nested $P$ term is defined by the subtraction (see the second line of Fig.~3M): \begin{equation}\label{eqP4a} (N1,P): \ \ \ G\, G^{(0)}\, G \ \to G\, G^{(0)}\, G - G^{(0)}\, G^{(0)}\, G^{(0)} - 2\,G^{(1)}\, G^{(0)}\, G^{(0)} \,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-124: In the accelerated scheme, we introduce additional subtractions that improve the partial-wave expansion (see the second line of Fig.~3M): \begin{equation}\label{eqP4b} (N1,P'): \ \ \ G\, G^{(0)}\, G \ \to \ldots - V_C\,\dot{G}^{(0)}\, G^{(0)}\, \dot{G}^{(0)}\, V_C - 2\,V_C\,\ddot{G}^{(0)}\, G^{(0)}\, G^{(0)}\, V_C \,,\end{equation} where $\ldots$ stand for the right-hand-side of Eq.~(\ref{eqP4a ).}
elements.sent-2411-125: In the standard scheme, the second nested $P$ term is defined by the subtraction (see the third line of Fig.~3M): \begin{equation}\label{eqP5a} (N2,P): \ \ \ G\, G^{(1)}\, G \ \to G\, G^{(1)}\, G - G^{(0)}\, G^{(1)}\, G^{(0)} \,.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-018: \begin{equation}\label{eqP5a} (N2,P): \ \ \ G\, G^{(1)}\, G \ \to G\, G^{(1)}\, G - G^{(0)}\, G^{(1)}\, G^{(0)} \,.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-126: In the accelerated scheme, we introduce an additional subtraction that improves the partial-wave expansion (see the third line of Fig.~3M): \begin{equation}\label{eqP5b} (N2,P'): \ \ \ G\, G^{(1)}\, G \ \to \ldots - 2\,V_C\,\dot{G}^{(0)}\, \dot{G}^{(0)}\, G^{(0)}\, V_C \,,\end{equation} where $\ldots$ stand for the right-hand-side of Eq.~(\ref{eqP5a ).}
elements.sent-2411-127: In addition, in the accelerated scheme we introduce the third nested $P$ term that takes care of the additional subtraction in Eq.~(\ref{eqM4 ) (see the fourth line of Fig.~3M): \begin{equation}\label{eqP5c} (N3,P'): \ \ \ G\, V_C\, \ddot{G}^{(0)}\, V_C \,G \ \to G\, V_C\, \ddot{G}^{(0)}\, V_C \,G - V_C\, G^{(0)}\, \ddot{G}^{(0)}\, G^{(0)} \,V_C \,.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-020: \begin{equation}\label{eqP5c} (N3,P'): \ \ \ G\, V_C\, \ddot{G}^{(0)}\, V_C \,G \ \to G\, V_C\, \ddot{G}^{(0)}\, V_C \,G - V_C\, G^{(0)}\, \ddot{G}^{(0)}\, G^{(0)} \,V_C \,.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-128: This contribution contains both the Dirac-Coulomb Green function $G$ and the UV diverging free self-energy operator.
elements.sent-2411-129: Its calculation is very similar to that of the first nested term.
elements.sent-2411-130: Similarly to the overlapping $F$ term, the nested $F$ term takes care of all subtracted term in the nested $M$ and $P$ terms.
elements.sent-2411-131: The corresponding diagrams are shown in the two bottom lines of Fig.~3M.
elements.sent-2411-132: Their calculation is performed by the technique developed for the $F$ term in our previous investigations \cite{yerokhin:03:epjd,yerokhin:10:sese,yerokhin:18:sese .. \end{widetext.}}
elements.section-2411-002: Supplemental Material: Details of the extrapolation procedure
elements.sent-2411-133: We now describe the details of our extrapolation of the all-order numerical results for the higher-order remainder $G_{60 (\Za)$ summarized in Table~I of the main text to lower values of $Z$.}
elements.sent-2411-134: The analytical form of the $\Za$ expansion of $G_{60 (\Za)$ is given by Eq.~(5) of the main text.}
elements.sent-2411-135: The main difficulty of the extrapolation arises from the presence of logarithmic terms in the $\Za$ expansion.
elements.sent-2411-136: The problem is that we cannot constraint the logarithmic coefficients based solely on the available numerical data.
elements.sent-2411-137: This is because the unknown logarithmic terms, such as $(\Za)\ln (\Za)^{-2}$, exhibit a distinct structure only for very small values $Z$, whereas for $Z>5$, where the numerical data is available, they are smooth functions that can be reasonably approximated by polynomials.
elements.sent-2411-138: As a result, if one uses a fitting function with logarithmic terms, the fitting minimization procedure does not have a well-localized minimum, and the resulting logarithmic coefficients cannot be obtained unambiguously.
elements.sent-2411-139: For this reason we choose to approximate the numerical data with polynomials only and incorporate the effects of missing logarithmic terms into the estimation of uncertainty, using the existing constraints derived from the $\Za$ expansion.
elements.sent-2411-140: Our extrapolation procedure is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-141: First, we subtract all known logarithmic contributions from the higher-order remainder $G_{60 (\Za)$, \begin{equation}G^{\prime}_{60}(\Za) = \ G_{60}(\Za) - B_{72}\,(\Za)\,L^2 \nonumber - B_{71}\,(\Za)\,L - B_{84}\,(\Za)^2\,L^4\,,\end{equation} where for $B_{71}$ we take the central value of the analytical estimate from Eq.~(6) of the main text.}
elements.sent-2411-142: We then extrapolate the numerical data for $G^{\prime _{60}$ and later re-add the subtracted logarithmic terms to obtain $G_{60}$.}
elements.sent-2411-143: Our extrapolation is performed into two steps.
elements.sent-2411-144: First, we approximate the numerical data for $G^{\prime _{60 (Z)$ with polynomials $P_n(Z)$ of degree $n = 2$, 3, and 4 and take the $P_3(Z)$ approximation as the central value.}}
elements.sent-2411-145: The uncertainty of the polynomial extrapolation is estimated as twice the maximal difference between the three fitting functions.
elements.sent-2411-146: In the second step, we evaluate additional uncertainties due to the presence of logarithmic terms.
elements.sent-2411-147: To do this, we subtract each of the unknown logarithmic terms in Eq.~(5) of the main text from $G^{\prime _{60}$, with coefficient given by the upper (lower) limit of the corresponding constraint in Eq.~(6) of the main text.}
elements.sent-2411-148: We then repeat the cubic extrapolation of the subtracted numerical data and then re-add the subtracted logarithmic term back.
elements.sent-2411-149: The difference with the unsubtracted cubic extrapolation gives us the estimate of the uncertainty due to this logarithmic term.
elements.sent-2411-150: We repeat this procedure for each of the unknown logarithmic terms and combine all uncertainties -- one due to the polynomial extrapolation and others due to four unknown logarithmic terms -- quadratically.
elements.sent-2411-151: The polynomial extrapolation is carried out by minimizing the following functional \begin{equation}\chi^2 = \sum_i \frac{[G^{\prime}_{60}(Z_i) - P_n(Z_i)]^2}{w_i^2}\,,\end{equation} where $Z_i = 8,\ldots,50$ and $w_i$ are weights.
elements.sent-2411-152: The data points with $Z = 5$, 6, and 7 were excluded because of their large uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-153: Inspired by Ref.~\cite{karshenboim:19:sese , we take the weights $w_i^2$ as the quadratic sum of the numerical error of $i$th data point and an additional effective error arising from the presence of terms scaling as $Z^k$ with $k > n$ in the numerical data.}
elements.sent-2411-154: Specifically, we take \begin{equation}w_i^2 = \delta_i^2 + (a\,Z_i^{n+1})^2\,,\end{equation} where $\delta_i$ is the numerical uncertainty of the data point $i$, $n$ is the degree of the fitting polynomial, and $a$ is some coefficient.
elements.sent-2411-155: The value of $a$ is selected by requiring that variations of the fitting result at $Z = 0$ caused by removal of the first two and the last two data points are small and approximately equal.
elements.sent-2411-156: Results of our extrapolation for the higher-order remainder $G_{60}$ and its uncertainty $\delta G_{60}$ are presented in Table~\ref{tab:fit .}
elements.sent-2411-157: The main sources of uncertainty are (i) the coefficient $B_{71}$, (ii) the coefficient $B_{82}$, (iii) the polynomial extrapolation.
elements.sent-2411-158: e.g., for $Z = 0$, the corresponding uncertainty contributions are 3.9, 2.7, and 2.4.
elements.figure-2411-006: \caption{Fitting results for the two-loop self-energy higher-order remainder $G_{60}$ and its uncertainty $\delta G_{60}$. \label{tab:fit}} \begin{ruledtabular} \begin{tabular}{l w{5.5} w{5.5} } \multicolumn{1}{c}{$Z$} \multicolumn{1}{c}{$G_{60}(Z)$} \multicolumn{1}{c}{$\delta G_{60}(Z)$} [-5pt] 0 -101.1370 5.5000 1 -104.0993 3.8027 2 -104.6048 2.8096 3 -104.4381 2.0909 4 -103.8999 1.5448 5 -103.1215 1.1219 6 -102.1758 0.7919 7 -101.1087 0.5330 8 -99.9510 0.3315 \end{tabular} \end{ruledtabular}
